skill.name: web3-testing
skill.description: Test smart contracts comprehensively using Hardhat and Foundry with unit tests, integration tests, and mainnet forking
skill.summary: Comprehensive smart contract testing toolkit for Hardhat and Foundry frameworks
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 63 patterns across 430 lines, but all findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is documentation containing code examples in markdown blocks, not executable code. Flagged patterns include standard Hardhat configuration (process.env usage), Solidity import syntax, and blockchain RPC endpoints - all legitimate web3 development practices. No malicious intent detected.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Code Examples
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown code blocks as executable code. The SKILL.md file contains instructional code examples for Hardhat and Foundry testing frameworks, delimited by markdown backticks. These are documentation, not executable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File contains only markdown documentation with code examples. No executable JavaScript or shell scripts present.

content.user_title: Test smart contracts with Hardhat and Foundry
content.value_statement: Smart contract developers need reliable testing frameworks to ensure code security and correctness. This skill provides comprehensive testing patterns using Hardhat and Foundry for unit tests, integration tests, and mainnet forking.
actual_capabilities: Generate Hardhat and Foundry test configurations for smart contracts
Create unit tests for Solidity contract functions and events
Set up mainnet forking for realistic integration testing
Implement fuzzing tests to find edge cases automatically
Configure gas optimization comparisons between implementations
Generate CI/CD workflows for automated test execution
limitations: Requires existing Solidity contracts to test
Mainnet forking requires valid RPC endpoint credentials
Does not replace formal security audits for production contracts
Test coverage depends on quality of test cases provided

use_cases.title: DeFi Protocol Testing
use_cases.description: Test decentralized exchange contracts with mainnet forking to verify swaps work correctly with real token contracts before deployment.
use_cases.target_user: DeFi developer launching a new AMM protocol

use_cases.title: NFT Collection Validation
use_cases.description: Validate NFT minting, transfer, and royalty distribution logic with comprehensive unit tests and fuzzing for edge cases.
use_cases.target_user: NFT project developer ensuring contract security

use_cases.title: Smart Contract Audit Preparation
use_cases.description: Generate comprehensive test suites with high coverage before submitting contracts for professional security audit.
use_cases.target_user: Blockchain startup CTO preparing for audit

prompt_templates.title: Basic Unit Test Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hardhat test file for my ERC20 token contract. Include tests for deployment, transfer functionality, and balance checking. Use the Token.sol contract at src/Token.sol.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner developer needs basic test structure for a standard token contract

prompt_templates.title: Foundry Fuzzing Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Foundry fuzzing test for my staking contract that validates rewards calculation across different stake amounts and time periods. Use vm.assume to set valid input ranges.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate developer wants to find edge cases with property-based testing

prompt_templates.title: Mainnet Fork Integration Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a mainnet fork test that interacts with the real Uniswap V2 router and DAI token. Test swapping 1 ETH for DAI and validate the received amount is within expected slippage.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced developer testing DeFi integration with real protocols

prompt_templates.title: Gas Optimization Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a test that compares gas usage between two implementations of the same contract function. Deploy both versions, execute identical operations, and report the gas difference with recommendations.

output_examples.input: Generate a Foundry test for a vault contract that handles deposits and withdrawals
output: Tests include: testInitialBalance, testDeposit, testWithdraw, testFailWithdrawMoreThanDeposit, testFuzzDepositAmount, testMultipleUsers, testVaultBalanceTracking, testEmergencyWithdraw
Coverage: 95% statement coverage across vault functions
Gas report: Average deposit costs 45000 gas, withdrawal costs 38000 gas

output_examples.input: Set up mainnet forking to test my lending protocol against Aave
output: Configured hardhat.config.js with mainnet forking settings
Created integration test connecting to Aave V2 lending pool at 0x7d2768dE32b0b80b7a3454c06BdAc94A69DDc7A9
Test validates borrowing against collateral at current mainnet rates
Fork block: 19500000, ETH balance mocked: 100 ETH for test address

best_practices: Use fixtures to set up test state and avoid code duplication between tests
Test both success paths and failure cases including revert conditions
Run tests with coverage enabled and aim for over 90 percent line coverage
anti_patterns: Testing only happy paths without checking edge cases and failure modes
Hardcoding private keys or RPC URLs directly in test files instead of using environment variables
Skipping mainnet fork tests for contracts that interact with external DeFi protocols

faq.question: What is the difference between Hardhat and Foundry for testing?
faq.answer: Hardhat uses JavaScript/TypeScript for tests with the Chai assertion library. Foundry uses Solidity for tests with built-in assertions. Foundry is faster and supports native fuzzing. Hardhat has a larger plugin ecosystem. Use Foundry for performance-critical testing and Hardhat for complex JavaScript-based test logic.

faq.question: How do I test contracts that interact with external protocols?
faq.answer: Use mainnet forking to create a local copy of mainnet state. Configure your RPC URL in hardhat.config.js or foundry.toml. Your tests can then interact with real deployed contracts like Uniswap, Aave, or Compound while running on your local network.

faq.question: What does fuzzing mean in smart contract testing?
faq.answer: Fuzzing automatically generates random inputs to test functions across many possible values. Foundry supports native fuzzing with testFuzz functions. It helps discover edge cases you might miss with manual test cases, such as boundary conditions or unexpected input combinations.

faq.question: How can I test time-dependent contract functions?
faq.answer: Use Hardhat's time helpers to increase blockchain time or Foundry's vm.warp to set specific timestamps. This allows testing of vesting schedules, timelocks, and other time-based logic without waiting for real time to pass.

faq.question: Is mainnet forking safe for testing?
faq.answer: Yes, mainnet forking runs entirely locally. No transactions are sent to mainnet. Your test transactions only exist in the local forked environment. However, never use real private keys with significant funds when testing, even locally.

faq.question: How do I measure test coverage for my smart contracts?
faq.answer: For Hardhat, run npx hardhat coverage to generate an HTML report showing statement, branch, and function coverage. For Foundry, use forge coverage for terminal output or forge coverage --report lcov for detailed reports. Aim for over 90 percent coverage before deployment.
